scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
justification: Meets all pass criteria: 8 business rules extracted with individual confidence via invariant classifications and testable assertions; 5 escalations surfaced as clarifying questions covering demand definition, guardrail values, price-lock policy, regulatory constraints, and competitive positioning; 'Demand' explicitly called out as undefined in escalation #1; acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written); overall confidence.level is 30 (<=40); no specific numeric values invented as requirements.

observations.text: Extracted 8 well-reasoned business rules, exceeding the minimum of 5, each with invariant classification and justification
Confidence level of 30 is appropriately low and well-justified — correctly identifies the input as a 'vision statement, not a specification'
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Demand ambiguity is prominently surfaced as the first escalation with concrete options for what it could mean
5 escalations function as clarifying questions covering demand signal, guardrails, price-lock, regulatory, and competitive positioning
7 edge cases identified including demand signal failure, price oscillation, and artificial demand inflation — shows deep domain thinking
Caught the word 'probably' before guardrails and flagged it as introducing doubt about whether guardrails are even confirmed
No specific values invented — all thresholds and parameters are left as undefined with placeholders like PRICE_FLOOR, PRICE_CEILING
The business_impact section is quite extensive for a spec this vague — borders on producing a 'complete domain analysis' though it stays appropriately qualified with uncertainty language

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 7 business rules extracted with individual invariant/confidence reasoning (≥5), 5 escalations functioning as clarifying questions plus additional edge cases (≥5), 'demand' explicitly called out as undefined in escalations and low_confidence_areas, acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written), overall confidence.level is 25 (≤40), and no specific values were invented as requirements (suggestions in recommendations are clearly labeled as such).

observations.text: Confidence level of 25 is well-calibrated for a CEO all-hands pitch with nearly every parameter undefined
Empty acceptance_criteria array is exactly correct — Bird correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Demand ambiguity is surfaced prominently as the first escalation with concrete options for resolution
7 business rules extracted exceeds the minimum of 5, each with invariant classification and justification
BR-5 (price > 0) and BR-7 (monotonicity) are excellent inferred rules beyond the obvious ones
Edge cases are strong — price quote honoring during checkout, demand flapping, regulatory price gouging, and demand feed failure are all high-value gaps
Recommendations in escalations are clearly framed as suggestions, not stated as requirements — no value invention
Stakeholder impact analysis identifies non-obvious affected groups like couriers, customer support, and legal/compliance
Business rules lack explicit per-rule confidence ratings (high/medium/low) — the rubric expected individual confidence on each rule, though invariant_justification partially compensates
The domain analysis is quite thorough for input this vague — borderline on 'producing a complete domain analysis as if input were fully specified' but saved by the heavy caveating throughout

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'ambiguity', got values: 'missing_context', 'missing_context'

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 business rules extracted (exceeds 5 minimum); 5 escalations surface clarifying questions covering demand definition, guardrail values, real-time semantics, regulatory review, and competitive-vs-profitable tension; 'demand' is explicitly called out as undefined in the first escalation and in low_confidence_areas; acceptance_criteria is empty (no Given/When/Then written); confidence.level is 30 (<=40); no specific numeric values were invented. Minor gap: rules lack per-rule numeric confidence ratings, but the confidence section categorizes high/low confidence areas that map to specific rules, achieving the same intent.

observations.text: Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
8 well-differentiated business rules extracted, exceeding the 5 minimum, including non-obvious implied rules like price-lock-on-acceptance (BR-7) and positive-price invariant (BR-6)
Demand ambiguity is the lead escalation with concrete options (absolute volume vs capacity ratio vs historical baseline vs composite)
confidence.level of 30 correctly reflects the vagueness of a casual CEO all-hands statement
10 low-confidence areas and 7 assumptions listed — thorough ambiguity surface area
7 edge cases identified including non-obvious ones like rapid demand oscillation, jurisdiction-aware guardrails, and quote-refresh UX trust issues
No specific threshold values invented — all testable assertions reference 'configured' values rather than fabricating numbers
Stakeholder impact analysis identifies 7 affected groups including non-obvious ones like couriers and sales/partnerships
Rules lack explicit per-rule confidence ratings (e.g., confidence: medium) — invariant flags partially compensate but don't directly express confidence
Missing explicit question about whether dynamic pricing applies to all services or a subset (partially covered by enterprise/contract mention in assumptions)
Missing explicit question about manual override capabilities (partially addressed in BR-4 but not escalated as a question)
